The patient appears agitated, is talking incoherently, and abruptly stops talking at times and starts another topic, and her skin is hot and dry. Which of the following disorders may a patient experiencing thyroid storm be erroneously diagnosed with due to overlapping symptoms?
rationale: When patients who experience thyroid storm, a severe, potentially life-threatening complication of hyperthyroidism (or thyrotoxicosis), present for emergency care, their symptoms are frequently mistaken for those of psychosis (or an acute mania), causing a delay in appropriate treatment. Many of the symptoms of thyroid storm are neurological in nature due to the intrinsic role the thyroid hormones play in supporting healthy nervous system function. Patients experiencing thyroid storm often present with symptoms of confusion, anxiety, agitation, talking incoherently, tremor or disorders of movement, among others. Non-neurological symptoms include hot, dry skin, complaint of palpitations, weight loss, exophthalmos, tachycardia or tachydysrhythmias, hyperthermia, and signs of heart failure.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: In the event of an open-water emergency landing in which the survivors are too far from shore to be able to reasonably swim, which of the following survival strategies may be the next best step?
rationale: If involved in an open-water landing while part of a medical air transport mission, all survivors should attempt to reach the shore. If this is not possible due to extreme far distance from shore or in the case of injured parties whose injuries prevent them from attempting to swim to shore, the next best step is for survivors to assume the HELP position in an attempt to try to prevent the development of hypothermia. HELP stands for heat escape-lessening posture and involves placing oneself in the fetal position with knees tucked up against the chest and arms crossed over the chest. A flotation device must be worn when using the HELP position. Flight helmets should be worn when in the water, as they provide a degree of insulation, and can aid in being spotted by rescuers. Survivors should then huddle together to, again, decrease development of hypothermia, and should signal to would-be rescuers. In addition, any life rafts should be carefully maintained.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are one of the CFRNs en route to the scene of a motorcycle crash via helicopter emergency medical service (EMS) transport. How should the scene evaluation begin?
options: With the use of night vision goggles (NVG) when bad weather is expected to interfere with transport
With the communications center gathering information about possible problems and circumstances the rescuers may confront
With the collection of information about the type of insurance that the victims may have so that an appropriate destination can be identified for the injured
With the evaluation of the landing area for obstacles that may impact the landing of the helicopter
rationale: The first steps in the evaluation of a scene include communication through a communications center about the location, number of victims, onsite providers, and possible injuries (problems and circumstances the rescuers may face), among other factors. Appropriate preparation for transport is needed to ensure safety for the transport team, rescuer, and patient during the transport process.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are transporting a previously healthy infant with a history of vomiting and diarrhea. During your assessment, you observe the infant responds only to painful stimulation, respiration rate is 42 bpm, and central pulses are rapid and weak. There are vesicular breath sounds, bilaterally, the patient has cool extremities, and a capillary refill time of more than five seconds. BP is 86/64 mm Hg, and glucose is 32 mg/dL. You administer 100% oxygen via face mask and start an IV. What is the most appropriate next treatment?
options: Infuse lactated ringer's solution 20 mL/kg over 1 hour
bolus D-10 W 20 mm/kg over 5 minutes
bolus of normal saline 20 mL/kg over 15 minutes, and D25W 2 to 4 mL/kg IV
bolus of D50W 0.45% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg over 15 minutes
rationale: The recommended treatment for children presenting with altered mental status begins with: 1. Assessment of the airway, breathing, and circulation 2. Immobilize cervical spine if there is suspected trauma 3. Initiate continuous pulse oximetry; with consideration for capnometry and administer oxygen if warranted 4. Provide dextrose for hypoglycemia - for children, 25% dextrose in water 2 mL/kg IV; for newborns, 5 mL/kg of 10% dextrose in water 5. Provide fluid resuscitation, 20 mL/kg of isotonic crystalloid, and repeat to total of 60 mL/kg as needed 6. Consider administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected sepsis or meningitis 7. Naloxone if opiate or clonidine overdose is suspected, 0.01 to 0.1 milligram/kg IV every 2 minutes 8. Flumazenil for suspected benzodiazepine overdose, 0.01 milligram/kg IV 9. Benzodiazepines for control of seizures (lorazepam, 0.1 milligram/kg IV; diazepam, 0.1 milligram/kg IV; or midazolam, 0.1 milligram/kg IV) 10. Prevent/treat hypothermia with heat lamps during resuscitation; treat hyperthermia

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Equipment to aid in the loading of a patient into a medical air transport aircraft may be obtained from which of the following sources?
rationale: After the patient has been packaged for medical air transport, the next step is the loading of the patient into the aircraft. The patient must be packaged in such a manner as to render the overall package as slight as possible in order to be able to actually insert the patient through the narrow aircraft door(s). The narrow character of the aircraft door(s) can pose an impediment to expeditious loading of the patient; with the increase of medical air transport, the development of specific equipment to facilitate in these situations has also increased. There are many manufacturers of specific equipment for loading of medical air transport which may be used to obtain the necessary equipment for this task.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Critical care transport personnel, particularly those working in rotor-wing aircraft, frequently participate in on-scene patient care. On-scene management guidelines include all the following, except:
options: Gain access to the patient only if properly trained
Never compromise rescuers to aid victims
Wear personal protective equipment (PPE) appropriate to the hazards on the scene
Report arrival to referring agencies and follow deployment instructions
rationale: Once the emergency aircraft has arrived safely, transport personnel must report their arrival to the Incident Command System (ICS), not to referring agencies, and follow deployment instructions; ICS is a management method designed to clarify command relationships during incidents. The other choices are correct.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient. While in flight, the patient begins to be confused, agitated, and restless. What should you do first to treat this patient?
rationale: These are all signs of hypoxia. The CFRN should first administer 100% oxygen and then continue to monitor for improving symptoms. A sedative could cause respiratory depression, making the problem worse. Giving a fluid bolus in this case will not help the patient. Only consider preparing for Rapid Sequence Intubation if you are unable to get the patient to oxygenate with the use of supplemental oxygen.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following possible anatomical sites which may be used for needle decompression in treatment of tension pneumothorax has been found to have the lowest predicted failure rate?
options: The 2nd intercostal space using the midclavicular line approach
The 3rd intercostal space using the midclavicular line approach
The 3rd intercostal space using the lateral midaxillary approach
The 4th intercostal space using the lateral midaxillary approach
rationale: When caring for a patient who has sustained a tension pneumothorax as a result of trauma, as part of establishing the ABCs should be needle decompression of the pneumothorax. A large-bore IV should be utilized for the needle decompression, and may be placed (on the affected side) in the 2nd intercostal space using the midclavicular line approach, or in the 4th or 5th intercostal space using the lateral midaxillary approach. Recent research has found that placement of the needle in either the 4th or 5th intercostal space using the lateral midaxillary approach has the lowest predicted failure rate across multiple population groups.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A patient presents with confusion, headache, and vision changes. Blood pressure has elevated from a baseline of 155/95 to 225/110. Which of the following is an appropriate first-line treatment?
rationale: Hypertensive emergency refers to elevation of systolic blood pressure greater than 225 mm Hg and/or diastolic blood pressure greater than 120 mm Hg that is associated with end-organ damage. Guidelines recommend reduction of the MAP by 25% within the first hour of treatment. Labetalol is the drug of choice in situations characterized by markedly elevated intracranial pressure, with an onset of action of 5-10 minutes. Duration of action is 3-8 hours. In cases of intracranial hemorrhage, treatment should commence if the initial systolic BP is above 220 mm Hg. Intensive reductions in systolic BP to below 140 mm Hg within the first 6 hours may be associated with harm (increased risk of renal complications). It may be appropriate to target the systolic BP to 140-179 mm Hg in patients who have a systolic BP between 150-220 mm Hg.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The medical transport team has received a patient who intentionally overdosed on his tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) drug. The CFRN would expect all the following assessment findings associated with this overdose, except:
rationale: There are several medications that have anticholinergic properties. TCA overdose is one of the most deadly poisonings, with morbidity and mortality statistics attesting to the mostly negative outcomes from overdose with these agents. Individuals who have overdosed on one of the TCA drugs typically experience the anticholinergic effects of the drug, including mydriasis, tachycardia, urinary retention, decreased gastrointestinal motility, and drying of the mucous membranes. Anticholinergic toxicity should be considered in any patient who presents with unexplained tachycardia, hyperthermia (not hypothermia), and altered mental status. Central nervous system symptoms include seizures, the presence of muscle jerking and twitching, confusion, agitation, hallucinations, seizures, and coma. Cardiac conduction changes also occur, most commonly the development of tachycardia with a prolonged QRS complex; prolongation greater than 100 ms should be treated by the administration of sodium bicarbonate.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The medical crew of an air transport program is providing care for a patient experiencing diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). They have administered an insulin bolus at a dose of 0.1 units/kg, and are preparing to establish a continuous infusion of insulin at 0.1 unit/kg/hour. The goal of the continuous insulin infusion should be to decrease the blood glucose level by:
rationale: The goal of treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is to treat the overwhelming hyperglycemia, provide fluid resuscitation, treat the metabolic acidosis, and restore electrolyte balance. The initial management of DKA always focuses on the use of intravenous fluids and intravenously administered short-acting insulin to reduce blood glucose. The administration of intravenous fluid alone is substantial enough to reduce blood glucose by 20 mg/dL to 50 mg/dL per hour. An initial insulin bolus dosed at 0.1 units/kg should be administered, followed by a continuous insulin infusion dosed at an additional 0.1 units/kg/hour. The goal of insulin administration should be to reduce the blood glucose by no more than 100 mg/dL/hour; more rapid reduction of blood glucose can contribute to the development of cerebral edema, obtundation, and headache. Once the blood glucose level reaches 200 mg/dL, it should be maintained at this level and the insulin infusion changed. An infusion of 5% dextrose with 0.02 to 0.05 units/kg/hour should be administered at that point.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following congenital heart defects (CHD) is considered to be a cyanotic heart disease?
rationale: TOF is a cyanotic heart defect with four anomalies including a ventricular septal defect (VSD), an overriding aorta arising from the VSD, pulmonary stenosis, and right ventricular hypertrophy. It is the most common form of cyanotic congenital heart disease and occurs slightly more often in boys. Hypoxic "tet" spells are characterized by sudden cyanosis and syncope; they are thought to be caused by a transient increase in right ventricular outflow obstruction, decreased systemic vascular resistance, decreased pulmonary blood flow, and increased right to left shunting. "Tet" spells should be treated with a knees-to-chest position, morphine, or fentanyl. ASD, VSD, and PDA are acyanotic heart diseases.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: An individual is traveling along the highway at a speed of 85-miles per hour (MPH) with one occupant in the vehicle when they lose control of the vehicle, causing the vehicle to leave the highway. The vehicle strikes a tree located along the side of the highway. The driver, who was wearing a seatbelt, hits the steering wheel and the air bag which is deployed by the impact and remains in the vehicle, but the passenger, who was not wearing a seatbelt, was ejected from the vehicle. Which of Newton's Laws of Motion best describes why the driver of the vehicle was not ejected from the vehicle?
rationale: Newton's three Laws of Motion can be used to explain the physics of many common trauma situations, and also the physical injuries sustained by the individuals involved in the trauma. During a motor vehicle accident, Newton's First Law of Motion comes into play when the vehicle crashes: "A body in motion remains in motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an outside force." Both the vehicle and the occupants of the vehicle remained in motion until some force acted against them; in the case of the vehicle, the tree, and in the case of the driver of the vehicle, the seatbelt. The passenger of the vehicle, who was not wearing a seatbelt, continued in forward motion until the force of the ground stopped his motion. The driver was not ejected from the vehicle as a result of Newton's Third Law of Motion: "For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction." In this case, both the airbag and the seatbelt exert its own force back onto the driver, causing a deceleration of the driver's forward motion. Newton's Second Law of Motion states, "Acceleration is dependent on the mass of the object and the force of the object." This law can be used to discuss the rate at which the passenger was ejected from the vehicle. Newton's Fourth Law is his law of gravitation, not motion.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are asked to evaluate one of your air transport flight members who is experiencing nausea and vomiting and had a witnessed seizure during the flight. Which of the following less common transport stresses has most likely contributed to the symptoms experienced by your flight team member?
rationale: Both air transport crews and their patients may experience the ill-effects of a host of stresses related to air transport. The most common stresses encountered during air transport include hypoxia, hyperventilation, changes due to barometric pressure, gastrointestinal changes (including abdominal distention, belching, and the passing of flatus), temperature changes, decreased humidity, noise, fatigue, aircraft vibration and motion, and changes related to gravitational forces and cabin pressure (decompression). Less common causes of air transport stress include spatial disorientation, fuel vapors, and flicker vertigo. Only flicker vertigo may cause the development of nausea and vomiting, as well as seizure activity and even loss of consciousness, all of which result from exposure to low-frequency flickering of light passing through rotor blades on the aircraft.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The compensatory stage of hypoxia occurs at what altitude?
rationale: The four stages of hypoxia are: The indifferent stage 0--10,000 ft. The compensatory stage 10,000--15,000 ft. The disturbance stage 15,000--20,000 ft. The critical stage 20,000--30,000 ft.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are providing medical air transport for a patient who has experienced cardiogenic shock and now has an intraaortic balloon pump (IABP) in place. During transport, you notice rust colored flakes in the tubing. What is the most likely significance of the rust colored flakes?
options: There are blood clots in the tubing
The helium tank has run out
The distal tip of the catheter has been dislodged
rationale: While the use of intraaortic balloon pumps (IABP) has gone down in recent years, medical air transport of a patient with an IABP continues with frequency. These patients are often critically ill, having experienced cardiogenic shock as a result of myocardial infarction (MI) with (most frequently) left- or right-sided heart failure, endocarditis, or an underlying heart defect such as hypoplastic left heart. The IABP serves to increase cardiac perfusion and carbon dioxide while, at the same time, decreasing the workload of the heart. And while medical air transport crews are typically well-trained in the care of, and transport of, patients requiring IABP, these devices are not without risk to the patient. In addition to the cardiac pathology that may occur to patients who have an IABP, including the development of ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia among other cardiac emergencies, the pump itself may experience complications which jeopardize the health and well-being of the patient. Power failure of the pump, running out of helium for the balloon, dislodgement of the catheter, oozing from the insertion site, occlusion of the catheter, and rupture of the balloon or balloon tubing are all potential complications of IABP placement. In the instance of balloon rupture, the medical air crew will most likely observe the presence of brown- or rust-colored flakes inside the tubing, indicating the presence of clotted red blood cells. Other signs of balloon rupture include the presence of frank blood in the tubing or a loss of gas alarm.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following types of diabetes insipidus (DI) is caused when urine is not properly concentrated by the kidneys?
rationale: Of the four types of DI, nephrogenic DI is caused when urine is not properly concentrated in the kidneys; it can be caused by genetics or it can also be acquired. Alterations in the kidneys account for acquired forms of the condition. This is most often a result of disease, drugs, or other conditions such as hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, pregnancy, and sickle cell anemia (SCA). Neurogenic DI is most often the result of decreased secretion of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from the posterior pituitary gland. Dipsogenic DI, also known as primary or psychogenic polydipsia, is caused by excessive fluid intake secondary to an abnormal thirst mechanism. Gestagenic DI is caused by a transient increase in the breakdown of ADH and can develop in pregnant women with eclampsia, preeclampsia, multiple gestation, or abnormal liver function.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Your patient develops sudden onset narrow-complex tachycardia at a rate of 208 bpm, with BP 128/56, EtCO₂ of 36 mmHg, and SpO₂ of 97%. Two large-bore IVs have been placed, and a 12-lead ECG shows SVT without evidence of ischemia/infarction. Vagal maneuvers have no visible effect. What is the next best step?
rationale: The 2020 AHA guidelines for ACLS state that for a regular narrow-complex tachycardia, without evidence of hypotension, altered mental status, signs of shock, or chest pain/discomfort, the next treatment options are vagal maneuvers, adenosine, administration of a β-Blocker or calcium channel blocker, and the consideration of obtaining expert consultation.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The pilot of a medical air transport en route in delivery of a patient has announced that the aircraft is experiencing significant engine problems and the flight should prepare for crash. All of the following procedures are standard as part of the pre-crash sequence except:
options: Assist in laying the patient flat on the floor and ask them to cross their arms across their chest if possible
Assume the crash position with knees together, feet 6 feet apart, and placed under the seat
Ensure helmets are strapped tightly with visor down
Turn off all oxygen in use, removing it from the patient
rationale: The design of all aircraft continues to evolve and change in an attempt to improve survival during aircraft crashes. Landing gear, fuel systems, and aircraft seat design are all aspects of the aircraft that have undergone significant improvements in recent years to ensure these systems specifically are more capable of withstanding the massive forces experienced during a crash. Aircraft seats are now designed to both absorb the energy of a crash and decrease the G forces experienced by the individuals occupying the seats. This being said, aircraft seats will break apart with a crash landing, and all passengers of the aircraft should ensure that when they assume the crash position, their legs are not positioned under the seats.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN called to the scene of a patient with burns to his face, arms, and upper torso region. You determine that the affected body surface area is 20 percent. You estimate that patients weight at 100 kg. You work for a company that has the Brooke Formula of fluid resuscitation in their protocols. How much fluid should you give the patient in the first 24 hours?
rationale: The Brooke Formula is 2 mL x kg x BSA = Total fluids in first 24 hours. According to the information in the question, this would be 2 mL x 100 kg x 20 percent BSA = 4000 mL. 2000 and 1000 are both too little volume for proper fluid volumes. 8000 mL would be the correct volume for Parkland Formula. The difference between Parkland and Brooke Formulas is Brooke is 2 mL x kg x BSA and Parkland is 4 mL x kg x BSA. So, Parkland is just twice the volume of Brooke. The way to remember this is Brooke requires 2 mL and Brooke starts with a "B" which is the 2nd letter of the alphabet.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following statements is most accurate in regard to the effects of G forces (gravitational forces) encountered during civilian medical air transport?
options: Research has been unable to definitely state if chronic exposure to G forces during civilian flight poses risk of the development of either short- or long-term physiologic changes
The G forces experienced by individuals who operate in medical air transport are insignificant and pose little risk of harm to the members of the air crew

The effects of the G forces encountered during a civilian medical air transport are significant and have been found to contribute to both short- and long-term physiologic changes in members of the air crew
Medical air crew member flight times are monitored and restricted to aid in preventing the development of long-term physiologic changes caused by the effects of G forces
rationale: While gravitational forces (G forces) are encountered during air travel, the G forces experienced by individuals who operate in medical air transport are insignificant, posing little to no risk of harm to the members of the air crew. Individuals who are most affected by G forces (typically high G forces) are those who are taking medications to control hypertension (particularly those taking beta blockers), or individuals who are dehydrated. The effects of G forces on the body causes a drop in blood pressure.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN caring for a trauma patient who is intubated and on the ventilator. The ventilator settings are: AC mode, rate 12 bpm, tidal volume of 400 mL, FiO₂ 1.0. What would be an indication that the patient is developing a tension pneumothorax?
options: Jugular vein distention and tracheal deviation
rationale: A sudden increase in PIP means that there is more resistance to the ventilator. This can be caused by a tension pneumothorax and should be further investigated to determine if this is the cause. Remembering the DOPE acronym for Dislodgement, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, Equipment failure will help in troubleshooting ventilator alarms. A decrease in PIP means there is loss of pressure in the system somewhere, possible dislodgment of the ET tube. Jugular vein distention and tracheal deviation are classic signs of pneumothorax; however, these are very late signs. The CFRN needs to know the signs and symptoms of a disease process early in its progression, not later. Muffled heart tones are signs of cardiac tamponade, not pneumothorax and, also, it is impossible to auscultate during flight in rotor wing aircraft.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: If the gag reflex is active, which cranial nerve is intact?
rationale: Cranial nerve VII (Facial nerve) supplies the muscles for facial movement, as well as facial proprioception. Cranial nerve VIII (Vestibulocochlear) has auditory and vestibular afferent components. Cranial nerves IX (Glossopharyngeal) and X (Vagus) are tested by observing pharyngeal musculature and gag reflexes. Cranial nerve XI (Accessory) is assessed by a shoulder shrug. Cranial nerve XII (Hypoglossal) controls lingual movement and can be assessed by asking the patient to stick out the tongue and observing for any asymmetry of motion.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: At the scene of an accident, the risk of a secondary incident involving property damage or personal injury to responding transport team members is real. Factors associated with secondary incidents include all the following, except:
rationale: Landing zones should be secured by law enforcement or fire departments; it is important to establish a traffic control zone once a transport has landed. The landing area may be of sufficient distance from the incident that it requires a second traffic control zone to be established. If this is the case, the transport team should consider themselves as part of two separate incidents, with a need to operate safely in both and to plan the transit between scenes carefully. Lack of training, lack of situational awareness, and inappropriate use of scene lighting are all factors associated with secondary incidents at the scene of an accident.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Battle's sign is most often associated with a:
rationale: Signs and symptoms associated with basilar skull fractures include cerebrospinal fluid leak, mastoid ecchymosis (Battle's sign), periorbital ecchymoses (raccoon eyes), hemotympanum, vertigo, decreased hearing or deafness, and seventh nerve palsy. Periorbital and mastoid ecchymoses develop gradually over hours after an injury. The presence of a basilar skull fracture is a significant risk factor for intracranial injury. Skull fractures are usually categorized by location (basilar vs. skull convexity), pattern (linear, depressed, or comminuted), and whether they are open or closed. A linear skull fracture is a break in a cranial bone resembling a thin line, without splintering, depression, or distortion of bone. A depressed skull fracture is a break in a cranial bone (or "crushed" portion of skull) with depression of the bone in toward the brain. Diastatic fractures occur when the fracture line transverses one or more sutures of the skull causing a widening of the suture, typically seen in children and infants, but can rarely occur in adults.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN called for the emergent transfer of a 30-year-old female who was the victim of an assault. The patient presented to the sending facility's ER with signs of shock. The sending RN states the patient is 36 weeks pregnant. Upon your initial assessment, while examining the abdomen, you note what appears to be fetal parts underneath the patient's skin. What do you suspect?
rationale: When able to palpate fetal parts underneath the mother's skin, the uterus has ruptured. Diaphragmatic rupture would result in tachypnea from decreased tidal volume, and the auscultation of bowel sounds in the lung fields. Ruptured liver would not cause the presence of fetal parts to be felt. Placenta abruptio would result in painful bleeding from the vagina.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: While evaluating a critically ill patient you are transporting by medical aircraft, you perform an electrocardiogram (ECG) and note the following patterns: U waves, inverted T waves, and depressed ST segment. Based on these findings, this patient is most likely experiencing which electrolyte abnormality?
rationale: A serum potassium level lower than 3.5 mEq/L (normally 3.5-5.0 mEq/L) indicates hypokalemia. A potassium deficit is an electrolyte imbalance that can potentially be life-threatening. Hypokalemia can be associated with cardiac conduction abnormalities, which are refractory to therapy until potassium levels are corrected. Electrocardiographic changes include shallow, flat, or inverted T waves, ST segment depression, and prominent U waves. Excessive diarrhea and vomiting, excessive diuresis, intestinal obstruction, GI suctioning, renal insufficiency, overuse of laxatives or steroids, severe trauma, and excessive diaphoresis are all associated with hypokalemia. It can also be a result of intracellular shifting of potassium caused by alkalosis.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are a medical air transport crew member participating in a mission involving a ground incident and are required to ride in a ground transport vehicle. Which of the following statements regarding traveling as a crew member in a ground transport vehicle is most accurate?
options: All crew members are required to wear a seat belt at all times
Only the patient is required to remain seat belted at all times in ground transport vehicles
All crew members are required to be trained in response to ground vehicle accidents
Training for response to ground vehicle accidents is not required for medical air transport crew
rationale: All medical air transport crew members should be trained in response to ground vehicle accidents in the event that they are required to participate in a ground vehicle mission. Both the patient and all crew members riding in the front of the ground transport vehicle are required to remain buckled in their seat belts at all times while the vehicle is in motion. If possible, crew members in the patient compartment should also remain buckled in their seat belts, but may need to remove their seat belts and move around the compartment in the provision of patient care.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: When communicating by radio within a medical air transport program, it is preferable to communicate:
options: Using generally accepted air transport codes
rationale: While it is obvious that specific language, jargon, and codes exist both within the air transport industry and the medical community at large, communication via radio within a medical air transport program becomes incredibly complex when one attempts to communicate using these specific languages. For purposes of preventing error and aiding in clear communication, it is preferable to communicate by radio using plain language.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following is the leading cause of blood transfusion related deaths?
rationale: TRALI causes acute pulmonary edema, and symptoms begin within just hours of starting the transfusion. Transfusion Associated Circulatory Overload can be managed by stopping the transfusion and giving Lasix. Anaphylactic reaction is easily treated by stopping the transfusion and then treating for anaphylaxis as normal. Febrile reaction can be contained with IV acetaminophen if no other symptoms are present.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following statements regarding benzodiazepine overdose is most accurate?
options: Flumazenil should be administered via rapid IV push to reverse the effects of the benzodiazepine overdose
Due to its narrow therapeutic window, a small error in benzodiazepine dosing can cause unintentional overdose
Patients who overdose on benzodiazepines often have several other poisons in their system at the time of overdose
Initial treatment of benzodiazepine overdose focuses on management of the patient's ABCs
rationale: Benzodiazepine overdose as a sole result of ingestion of the benzodiazepine does not occur with nearly as much frequency as does benzodiazepine overdose as a result of polypharmacy. Quite a large dose of benzodiazepine must be ingested for overdose to actually occur as a direct result of the benzodiazepine. Most benzodiazepine-related poisonings occur as a result of the individual having multiple poisons present in his system at the time, including alcohol, the tricyclic antidepressants, etc. The first responder treating an individual with a known benzodiazepine overdose must treat with caution due to this likelihood of multiple poisons being in the patient's body, as the patient may suffer severe negative effects as a result of administration of the antidote (flumazenil), particularly severe seizures. If flumazenil is administered, it should be administered slowly and in small doses in an attempt to prevent the development of seizures; rapid administration of flumazenil itself can cause seizures. If the patient has ingested multiple substances, the seizure threshold may be significantly lowered in the face of flumazenil administration.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: When a nurse is developing a checklist to be used for transport, which of the following should be considered?
options: Five items or fewer should be listed in each workflow section.
The length should be one page or shorter.
Checklists should not be modified once they are implemented.
Checklists should be printed on brightly colored paper for attention.
rationale: A checklist is not a teaching tool or algorithm but a performance aid. Some traits of a well-written transport checklist include the following: Each workflow section should not contain more than 10 items. Checklists on colored paper may not be clearly copied, so minimal colors or basic colors should be utilized (if any). Checklists are more useful on single pages and should be modified as needed. The font should be easily readable.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A 14-year-old male who was involved in an all-terrain vehicle (ATV) accident is being airlifted for medical transport to a trauma center 50 miles away from the site of the accident. Which of the following basic trauma imaging modalities can be used in the field by field trauma providers to aid in rapid identification of trauma injuries not apparent on the primary assessment?
rationale: FAST, eFAST, and RUSH examinations all involve use of portable sonography in rapid evaluation of trauma patients to aid in better diagnosis and subsequently improved interventions in these patients. Focused Assessment with Sonography (FAST) is now regularly used in the field by transport providers to aid in identifying traumatic injuries that are not apparent to visual examination of the patient. FAST, while not technically a part of the primary trauma assessment, is considered to be an adjunct to the primary assessment and may be utilized during the primary assessment by field providers trained in use of the technique. FAST may be used to identify the presence of blood in the pericardium (tamponade) and three spaces in the peritoneal cavity. The eFAST examination is an extension of the FAST examination, and is most likely to be performed in the emergency department by the (ED) physician or by a surgeon prior to surgery, versus in the field. The RUSH examination is also a sonographic examination and was developed for use in the ED to evaluate patients experiencing hypotension, the cause of which had not yet been determined. RUSH stands for Rapid Ultrasound for Shock and Hypotension. XRAY is not a modality typically utilized in the field.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following statements regarding myxedema coma is most accurate?
options: Death most often occurs a result of respiratory failure
Cardiovascular collapse is the primary complication of myxedema coma
Hyperpyrexia should not be treated with the administration of salicylates
rationale: Myxedema coma is a potentially deadly complication of untreated hypothyroidism. It most frequently occurs during the winter months and is most often encountered in patients over the age of 60. In patients who are truly comatose as a result of myxedema, the tongue may fully or partially obstruct the airway, and both respirations and respiratory drive are weak or ineffective. Pulmonary infection may result, as may a build-up of carbon dioxide, due to the hypoventilation which impairs to the level of the alveoli. Patients with hypothyroidism may often be overweight or obese, which can contribute to the further respiratory complication of sleep apnea, worsening the respiratory status. Patients who die as a result of myxedema coma most often do so as a result of respiratory failure. Glomerular filtration rate is decreased in myxedema coma, as is renal blood flow. Patients with myxedema coma most often experience hypothermia, not hyperthermia (hyperpyrexia). The hyperthermia of thyroid storm should not be treated with salicylates. Cardiovascular complications include low cardiac output, decreased heart rate, and decreased stroke volume.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient suffering from decompression sickness. He is confused, has visual disturbances, and has had seizures prior to your arrival. What type of decompression sickness is the patient experiencing?
rationale: Type 2 decompression sickness is defined by neurological changes. Type 1 is associated with bone and joint pain. While there are several types of decompression sickness, the aeromedical provider usually is only concerned with type 1 and type 2.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Per National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) and Air Surface Transport Nurses Association (ASTNA), recommendations on the use of personal protective equipment (PPE) for air medical crew members participating in helicopter missions, all of the following items are required except:
rationale: Just as the use of PPE is required when providing care to patients who present risk of the transmission of infectious agents (or for those who are at increased risk of becoming infected), so specific PPE is required for all medical crew members participating in medical air transport or rescue on helicopter missions. Long-sleeved fire retardant flight suits, leather or Nomex boots, and visored helmets are all required components of the PPE for medical crew members of helicopter transport teams. Eye shields and/or goggles are not required PPE components for helicopter transport crews.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Four stages of hypoxia must be considered when examining its effects on human pathophysiology. Which stage occurs at an altitude of 10,000 to 15,000 feet?
rationale: The four stages of hypoxia are divided by altitude: 1. Indifferent stage: this physiologic zone starts at sea level and extends to 10,000 feet; the body reacts to the lessened availability of oxygen in the air with a slight increase in heart rate and ventilation. 2. Compensatory stage: occurs from 10,000 to 15,000 feet; the body attempts to protect itself against hypoxia by increasing BP, heart rate, and depth and rate of respiration occurs. 3. Disturbance stage: occurs between 15,000 and 20,000 feet; characterized by dizziness, sleepiness, tunnel vision, and cyanosis. Thinking becomes slowed, and muscle coordination decreases. 4. Critical stage: occurs between 20,000 and 30,000 feet and features marked mental confusion and incapacitation followed by unconsciousness (usually within a few minutes).

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following statements about the use and functioning of an intraaortic balloon pump (IABP) during medical air transport is most accurate?
options: During ascent to altitude, the IABP needs to be manually purged with helium
Refilling of the balloon with helium occurs automatically every 2 hours during medical air transport
During medical air transport, the balloon requires manual filling by the medical crew due to changes in balloon volume due to altitude changes
Use of the IABP during medical air transport requires that triggers and timing be controlled manually by the medical crew
rationale: Intraaortic balloon pumps (IABPs) continue to be frequently encountered by the medical crews of medical air transport programs as they continue to transport critically ill patients to medical centers which provide the highest level of cardiac care. The IABP may be placed in automatic mode or manual mode during air transport. If it is placed in manual mode, the medical crew will be required to select and control all the triggers and timing of triggers during flight, as well as manually fill the balloon with helium to adjust for the atmospheric pressure changes which affect the gas. During manual control of balloon filling, the team will need to fill the balloon every 2,000 feet during ascent and every 1,000 feet during descent. The most modern versions of IABPs typically have an automatic balloon refill setting, even when the pump is placed in manual mode, and the balloon is refilled every 2 hours, as well as more frequently based on changes noted by volume sensors.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You respond to a 911 call from a 22-year-old primagravida female who is 38 6/7 weeks pregnant. She was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 28 weeks. She is currently at home and states her water broke approximately 15 minutes ago. She is experiencing excruciating back pain. If you have to deliver the infant during transport, which of the following complications should you expect?
rationale: Infants born to mothers with gestational diabetes are at increased risk for shoulder dystocia, as these infants tend to be large for gestational age (LGA). With shoulder dystocia, the head delivers but the anterior shoulder pushes against the symphysis. In most situations, the head is the largest diameter of the fetus; occasionally, the shoulders have a larger diameter. The obvious concern is that the head will fit through the pelvic bones and the shoulders will be unable to do so. The McRoberts maneuver is the most common intervention in cases of shoulder dystocia and involves flexing the mother's knees against her chest during the next contraction and applying suprapubic pressure. Complications of shoulder dystocia include brachial plexus damage and fractured fetal clavicle. Fetal hypoxia can occur when the cord is drawn into the pelvis and compressed.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are a medical crew member of a medical air transport program that frequently flies missions over the Great Lakes. All of the following statements regarding requirements for medical air transport programs which operate over large bodies of water are correct except:
options: All crew members are required to wear a personal flotation device (PFD)
All crew members are required to know how to swim
All crew members are required to be familiar with manual activation of the strobe light attached to the aircraft
All crew members must be trained in open-water survival techniques
rationale: Any medical air transport program that frequently flies missions over large bodies of water are required to have procedures and plans in place in the event of a forced water landing, often referred to as ditching. All crew members are required to know how to swim, and must wear a PFD for the duration of flights over large bodies of water. The PFD should be equipped with an attached strobe light which will activate on contact with water. Some programs may also require the crew members to wear additional survival gear or vest systems which contain alternate signaling mechanisms. Crew members must be trained in open-water survival techniques, as well as thoroughly trained in exiting an aircraft which has crashed in, or been forced to make an emergency landing in, the water. Note: The strobe light activates automatically on contact with water --- crew members are not required to be familiar with manual activation. This is the EXCEPT answer.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Where can first responders quickly locate general information about hazardous materials when responding to an incident in the field?
options: In the possession of the operations manager
rationale: In each first response vehicle, including medical air transport aircraft, is kept the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), in which is contained general information about hazardous material. This emergency text is divided into six sections, each section of which deals with a different component of response to a hazardous materials (hazmat) incident. The ERG should only be used for reference during the initial stage of response to a hazmat incident in an attempt to keep both the general public and first responders safe.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Your patient is being weaned from the ventilator and is struggling to overcome the resistance generated by the endotracheal tube (ETT). Which of the following ventilator modes can be utilized to assist the patient in this scenario?
rationale: During PSV, the patient is able to initiate a breath, which is then supported by a preset level of pressure delivered by the ventilator. The patient is in control of all the other aspects of breathing, including the rate of breaths, the rate or length of inspiratory time, and the tidal volume. Pressure support aids the patient in overcoming the resistance caused by the presence of the ETT and is often employed when the patient is being weaned from the ventilator.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are providing care to an 11-month-old male with symptoms consistent with an upper respiratory tract infection. He has a temperature of 38.0 degrees Celsius, a runny nose, and inspiratory stridor during respirations. During your time caring for the patient, he develops a barking cough, and you suspect the patient has croup. Which of the following organisms is most likely to cause croup?
rationale: One of the most common childhood upper airway viral infections is that of croup. It most commonly affects children between the ages of six months and three years, but frequently affects older children as well. Croup is typically distinguished by its barking cough which is worse at night and tends to resolve during daylight hours. Children with croup often have symptoms typical for upper respiratory tract infections, including low-grade fever, runny nose, a hoarse voice, and swollen mucous membranes in the nose. The presence of inspiratory stridor may be present, and if the condition worsens, the patient may experience respiratory distress with chest retractions noted. Croup is most often caused by parainfluenza virus type 1, and less commonly parainfluenza virus type 3.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A patient who is in moderate congestive heart failure is being transported via medical air transport. The patient is receiving non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NPPV) using CPAP, and has also received two doses of nitroglycerin. The patient is secured in supine position for takeoff of the aircraft and, shortly after takeoff, experiences cardiac arrest. Typically, during cardiac arrest which takes place during air transport, how many members of the air transport crew are able to participate in the resuscitation using advanced life support?
options: All the crew members, minus the pilot
rationale: All the members of the medical air transport crew are expected to obtain and maintain their certification both in basic life support (BLS) skills and advanced cardiac life support (ACLS) skills. While, due to the closing of hospitals in some rural areas and staffing shortages in other areas, the medical air transport crews may now be transporting fewer critically ill patients than previously and be seeing an increase in the transport of more stable patients, the risk of cardiac arrest during air transport remains high. Providing ACLS in the cramped space of an aircraft poses many challenges, among them, a limit to the number of crew members who can actively participate in the provision of ACLS at any one time (2). The medical crew members must be intimately familiar with their role expectations during cardiac arrest to ensure effective delivery of ACLS.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN caring for a recently intubated trauma patient. The patient's vital signs are HR-104 sinus rhythm, BP-143/87, respirations-12 (ventilated), and SPO₂ of 100%. Which medication should you consider for post intubation management?
rationale: Ensuring adequate sedation for a patient post intubation is essential. Ketamine is the only answer choice that has analgesic properties and is a sedative. Etomidate is used as an induction agent for its quick onset; however, the medication's half-life is very short, making it less than ideal to maintain sedation. Also, Etomidate has no analgesic properties. While the patient may be unresponsive, they are still able to experience pain and discomfort. Succinylcholine and Rocuronium are both neuromuscular blocking agents and have no analgesic or anesthetic properties. In fact, there have been reported instances when patients are administered only NMBA and recall every part of the intubation procedure.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A 49-year-old male who was elk hunting with his teenage son was accidentally shot at close range when his son was preparing to exit the hunting stand. All of the following internal organs are better able to tolerate temporary cavitation from gunshot wounds, except:
rationale: Penetrating injuries, such as firearm injuries or stab injuries, all involve the formation of a cavitation by the object that penetrates the tissues. A temporary cavity is formed by the transference of the kinetic energy from the object, in this case the bullet, to the tissues, causing a stretching of the soft tissues. A permanent cavity is formed from necrosing of the damaged tissue. Elastic tissues, such as the lungs, the bowel walls, and the muscles, are better able to tolerate this stretching force than are the more solid tissues, such as the liver and spleen.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: While transporting a patient who has an Impella Ventricular Support System device in place, the "suction event" alarm sounds. What step(s) should the medical transport crew take in resolving the issue which caused the sounding of the alarm?
options: Decrease the RPMs and administer intravenous (IV) fluid
Begin providing flow through use of the manual hand crank
Apply pressure to the catheter insertion site
rationale: Impella Ventricular Support Systems are catheter-based devices which are used to treat the cardiac failure associated with cardiogenic shock which persists after cardiac surgery or myocardial infarction (MI). These ventricular assist devices (VADs) may be used to support failure of either the left or right ventricle, and are typically inserted via the femoral arteries, with right ventricular failure being treated by placement of the catheter in the pulmonary artery, and left ventricular failure being treated by placement of the catheter directly into the left ventricle after being advanced through the ascending aorta. The device works to support ventricular function by use of an impeller which, through the use of centrifugal force and an increasing number of RPMs, pumps blood. The RPM speed is referred to as "P levels," which range from 10,000 RPM (the lowest and slowest speed) to 33,000 RPM. The P level should not be adjusted by the medical air transport crew, except in the sounding of a "suction event" alarm, or if the patient requires cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Suction events occur as a result of a decrease in the filling of the pump (a decrease in preload) and the pump is offloading blood at the same rate. This results in a portion of the ventricular wall being sucked over the inlet cannula of the pump, which then triggers the pump to decrease the RPMs in an attempt to release the suction. The medical transport crew should decrease the P level accordingly to facilitate this release of suction.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What is the most common type of shock experienced by children?
rationale: Hypovolemic shock is the most commonly experienced type of shock in children; it develops when the intravascular volume is insufficient to maintain tissue perfusion. The decrease in intravascular volume results in a decreased preload, which decreases cardiac output, leading to an increase in SVR and increased capillary refill. Sources of volume loss include vomiting; diarrhea; osmotic diuresis; capillary leak from sepsis; and intraabdominal processes with third space losses such as pancreatitis, intussusception, appendicitis, burn injuries, hemorrhage, inadequate fluid intake, or insensible losses.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: "The volume of a gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas." This is which law?
rationale: The equation for Charles' Law is V₁V₂=T₁T₂. As temperatures decrease, the volume of a gas also decreases and vice versa. This is why it is easier for rotor wing aircraft to fly in colder weather when the air is denser. Boyle's Law relates gas volume to pressure changes. Henry's Law relates what amount of gas is able to dissolve into a liquid. Fick's Law describes the diffusion of gas across a membrane with changing pressures.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are air transporting a patient who requires endotracheal intubation and determine that the patient has a difficult airway. Which of the following mnemonics can be used to assist in deciding if the patient would benefit most from intubation using video-assist or use of direct laryngoscopy?
rationale: Just as it is not uncommon for the medical air crew member to need to intubate a patient they are transporting, it is also not uncommon to encounter a difficult airway. Direct laryngoscopy (DL) is typically used when attempting to perform intubation but may not be adequate in the case of a difficult airway, making a video-assisted intubation more likely to be successful. During video-assisted intubation, a special laryngoscope with a video-transmitting device and monitor is used to more clearly visualize the airway. The blade on the laryngoscope has an increased angle, further aiding in the endotracheal tube (ETT) placement. The medical air transport crew member can use the mnemonic HEAVEN to help decide between use of DL or a video laryngoscopy (VL). H: Hypoxemia. While DL is more rapid to perform, VL may hasten intubation if the patient has a difficult airway. E: Extremes of size (referring to patient size). Pediatric patients benefit from use of a straight blade (DL), while obese patients benefit from the slow advancing of the ETT used during VL. A: Anatomic disruption or obstruction. The use of VL can be advantageous on the out-to-in view, while DL is preferable from the in-to-out view. V: Vomit (blood or fluid) in airway. If the vomitus is bloody or the vomiting is uncontrollable, DL is preferable. E: Exsanguination. While DL is more rapid to perform, VL may hasten intubation if the patient has a difficult airway. N: Neck mobility. The VL allows for more gentle intubation. MALLAMPATI is not a mnemonic, it is a grading system to determine potential ease or difficulty of intubation based on visual criteria of the patient's airway. The BURP mnemonic stands for "backward, upward, and rightward pressure," and describes the pressure that has been placed on the airway during external manipulation of the airway during intubation. This technique is no longer typically recommended for use during routine intubation. The ROMAN mnemonic stands for "radiation/restriction, obesity/obstruction/obstructive sleep apnea, mask seal/male sex/Mallampati, age, and no teeth," and is used when determining if a patient may prove difficult to ventilate by use of a bag-valve mask device.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Several basic types of radio systems are used in medical transport communications systems. Which of the following provides the ability to transmit and receive simultaneously with two frequencies?
rationale: The radio continues to be the key hardware element in medical transport communications systems. A full duplex system transmits and receives simultaneously with two frequencies (usually UHF). A half duplex system transmits or receives in one direction at a time with two frequencies (typically UHF high band). A multiplex system transmits from two or more sources over the same frequency. A simplex system transmits in one direction at a time with a single frequency.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: After administration of first-line medication treatment for acute coronary syndrome (ACS), all of the following medications may be used as next steps in treatment of ACS, except:
rationale: Acute coronary syndrome (ACS), which encompasses three acute cardiac events (unstable angina, non-ST elevation myocardial infarction \[NSTEMI\], and acute ST elevation myocardial infarction \[STEMI\]), results from atherosclerotic plaque rupture within the coronary vessels, and may ultimately lead to the patient's death from sudden cardiac death. Patients who are believed to be experiencing an ACS event should be rapidly evaluated and provided with initial treatment, which includes the administration of oxygen, nitroglycerin, aspirin, and morphine (some programs do not use morphine in treatment of the chest pain associated with ACS, and simply rely on nitroglycerin for this). Once initial treatment has been administered, additional medications may be used to continue treatment of ACS. These include the administration of beta blocker medications, such as metoprolol, to decrease myocardial oxygen consumption; heparin, to reduce the further formation of thromboses (heparin does not dissolve already established clots); and a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor, such as abciximab, which inhibits platelet aggregation.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following statements regarding emergency locator transmitters (ELT) on emergency medical services (EMS) aircraft is most accurate?
options: The ELT can be activated manually or automatically
The ELT transmission is broadcast on distress frequency 121.5 MHz which is monitored by the COSPAS-SARSAT search and rescue satellite system
The ELT is automatically activated in response to an impact of 3 Gs or less

The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) requires that the ELT transmit its distress signal on frequency 405 MHz, which provides information about the aircraft's global positioning system (GPS) location
rationale: The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) requires all EMS aircraft to have an emergency locator transmitter (ELT) on board in the incident of aircraft crash. Each crew member, including the members of the medical air transport crew, should know the position of the ELT(s) and should be instructed on how to activate the ELT manually if it is not automatically activated by the crash. ELTs should automatically activate in response to a crash that exceeds 4 G forces, but this should be confirmed by a crew member, not just assumed. Distress signals are broadcast over frequencies 121.5 MHz and 406 MHz; distress frequency 121.5 MHz is no longer monitored by the COSPAS-SARSAT (an international humanitarian search and rescue organization) search and rescue satellite system.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Identifying the need to transfer a patient to a different hospital and determining the most appropriate mode of transport are based on several factors. Which of the following is not one of these factors?
options: The type of health insurance the patient holds
The severity of the patient's condition
rationale: The need to transport a patient may be because of the lack of availability of necessary resources at the treating facility, the availability of higher-level care with improved outcomes at other facilities, or the desire to move the patient to facilities closer to home or support systems for continued care. Several factors are taken into consideration and include: The severity of the patient's illness or injury Transport time, distance, terrain, and weather The need for nursing and medical expertise, diagnostic procedures, or other specialty services not available at the referring healthcare facility A request by the patient's family for transfer to another facility Identifying the type of insurance the patient has prior to transport is not a factor when identifying the need to transfer a patient.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Following an emergency landing of a medical air transport aircraft in wilderness terrain, the crew prioritizes hydration after ensuring scene safety and patient stabilization. Which of the following should not be consumed directly as a source of hydration due to the risk of heat loss and hypothermia?
options: Standing surface water such as puddles
Condensation collected from the aircraft windshield
rationale: Snow should not be consumed directly as a hydration source because ingesting cold snow lowers core body temperature and increases the risk of heat loss and hypothermia. In wilderness survival situations, snow must be melted prior to consumption to reduce thermoregulatory stress and preserve body heat. Dew collected from plant surfaces can be used as a hydration source when collected properly and purified, making it an acceptable option in survival situations despite contamination risk. Standing surface water such as puddles may be used for hydration if appropriately treated using filtration, boiling, or chemical purification methods, even though untreated water carries infectious risk. Condensation collected from surfaces such as the aircraft windshield can be used as a hydration source when collected and purified, as it does not pose the same immediate thermoregulatory risk as consuming snow directly.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A landing zone should generally be what size?
rationale: Generally 100 ft x 100 ft is a large enough landing zone. The other options are too small.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Upon arriving at the scene of a rollover motor vehicle crash (MVC), you discover a gun lying close to the vehicle. What should you do with the weapon?
options: Handling it gently, move it to a safe location and call law enforcement
Leave it in the same position in which it was discovered and call law enforcement
Handling it gently, transport it with the patient as it may be evidence
Handling it gently, ensure the weapon is unloaded and call law enforcement
rationale: A weapon found at the scene of an emergency must be left in the exact position in which it was discovered. Rescuers should assume the weapon is loaded and in operating order even if it appears otherwise. Law enforcement officers should be called. If the weapon must be moved for any reason before police officers arrive, the task should be delegated to a trustworthy person; only one person should handle the weapon until it can be turned over to law enforcement. A photograph should be taken of the weapon in place, with reference points, before moving it for any reason.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Situational awareness (SA) is best characterized by which of the following?
options: Knowing what is happening around you in such a way that you can anticipate near-future events
Comprehending what is happening in a patient care situation using current data related to the patient's problem
Comprehending what is happening solely with the patient's situation
Having the location of the aircraft or ground vehicle known at all times during transport to ensure the safety of all involved
rationale: SA refers to the maintenance of an active awareness of all aspects of the transport environment. This awareness includes scanning for other aircraft, listening to radio traffic, maintaining a sterile environment during critical phases of transport, and observing for hazards on approach to scenes or other unfamiliar transport areas. This allows you to anticipate near-future events by perceiving information in the environment, comprehending that information accurately, and using it to construct a model of what is about to happen.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: When must seat belts be worn by crew members?
options: Only during inadvertent Instrument Meteorological Conditions (IMC).
Crew members do not have to wear seat belts because it interferes with patient care
At all times except straight and level flight unless directed by the pilot in charge (PC/PIC)
Only during take offs and landings
rationale: The only time it is acceptable to not wear your seat belt is during straight and level flight unless the PC directs you otherwise. Crew members must wear seat belts during all critical phases of flight and at the discretion of the PC.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: During air transport of a patient, you begin experiencing severe pain in an upper front tooth during ascent. Which of the following are you most likely experiencing?
rationale: Barodontalgia, also referred to as aerodontalgia, is a dental condition which results from the effects of barometric pressure changes on teeth with diseased pulp. Typically, an individual will experience moderate to severe tooth pain during ascent during air transport, and the pain can be localized to a specific tooth. Symptoms tend to resolve once the individual begins descent. Patients or crew members who have cavities which are unfilled or poorly filled, or unrecognized dental disease, may experience the symptoms of barodontalgia; in individuals who have fillings, air which is trapped in the filled space expands during ascent as a result of Boyle's Law. Excellent dental care is a must for those involved in air transport. Barosinusitis causes dental symptoms similar to those of barodontalgia, however the dental symptoms of barosinusitis occur during both ascent and descent, and typically affects the upper posterior teeth. The symptoms of delayed ear block occur after a transport flight has been completed, and occur as a result of breathing 100% oxygen. Barotitis media is also referred to as ear block, and occurs during air transport as a result of negative pressure within the middle ear, creating a vacuum.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Who has the final authority in regard to flight related issues?
rationale: The Pilot in Command has the final authority when it comes to flight related issues. During flight, not even the company's medical director can override the Pilot in Command when it comes to flight related issues.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: You are the CFRN assigned to a transport team en route to the scene of a rollover motor vehicle accident (MVA) by rotor-wing aircraft. Prearrival considerations include all the following components, except:
rationale: Prearrival/en route considerations include communication through a communications center about the location, terrain, number of victims, weather conditions, and time of day, as well as information about any fire, spilled fuel, toxic chemicals, overturned or entangled vehicles, and downed electrical lines. Collecting information about the type of insurance that the victims may have is not an important consideration at this point.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following descriptors most accurately describes the term "sterile cockpit"?
options: Requirement for pilot to wear all appropriate PPE when transporting immunocompromised patients
Restricting intercom communication during takeoff and landing
Limitations placed on intercom usage during straight or level flight
Requirement for all cockpit crew to follow sterile procedure when transporting immunocompromised patients
rationale: The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) is responsible for the regulation of all the details pertaining to civilian flights, including medical air transport. The FAA requires that a sterile cockpit be maintained during all critical phases of the flight: takeoff, landing, during refueling, and during taxiing either on the ground or in the air. Intercom communication throughout the aircraft is restricted during these critical phases, and normal intercom communication can be resumed once straight or level flight is resumed.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: An effective comprehensive safety management system (SMS) should be a major part of all transport programs. All the following are components of an SMS, except:
options: system defect allowing operating errors to exist
system to track and document root cause analysis
system to audit and review policy and procedures
rationale: A system defect that allows operating errors to exist is innate to the structure of any system. This is not a designed component of an SMS. The Accreditation Standards published by the Commission on Accreditation of Medical Transport Systems (CAMTS) list the components of an SMS, which include a system to track and document root cause analysis, a system to audit and review policy and processes, and a designated safety committee, among others.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The SBAR is a checklist that can ensure completeness and consistency during the patient handoff process. This form of communication has all the following components, except:
rationale: SBAR is a simple mnemonic that includes the following: Situation (not safety concerns): a brief summary of what is going on with the patient (past and present); Background: medical history and information related to the patient's current problem; Assessment: what the caregiver has determined to be the patient's current problem; Recommendations: what interventions have been performed and may need to be performed for the patient.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Emergency radio calls should only be made at whose direction?
rationale: Emergency communications are ultimately the responsibility of the pilot but may be delegated to a crew member, depending on the policy program. If a crew member is expected to make emergency radio calls, this responsibility should be clearly defined in the program's emergency procedures; they should only be made at the direction of the pilot. All members of the flight team should be familiar with radio operations.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What is considered to be the most dangerous component of a helicopter?
rationale: The most obvious component of the helicopter that presents a risk is the rotor system, and the tail rotor is potentially the most hazardous component of the helicopter. It becomes nearly invisible when in motion, with speeds greater than 2000 rpm. A safety person should be designated at all unsecured landing sites to ensure that no one inadvertently walks near the tail rotor. All individuals who approach the helicopter must do so in full view of the pilot and should not proceed under the rotor disk without the pilot's permission. The safety approach zone for most helicopters is from the sides (at the 3 o'clock or 9 o'clock position).

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What is the accrediting body for medical transport companies?
options: National Highway Traffic and Safety Administration
rationale: CAMTS is an acronym for Commission for the Accreditation of Medical Transport Systems. The Joint Commission accredits hospitals. The National Highway Traffic and Safety Administration is not an accrediting body, nor is the Department of Transportation.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which is not considered a critical phase of flight?
rationale: Communication is a key aspect of safe and efficient functioning in high-risk environments; however, accident investigations have shown that too much communication can create hazardous distractions for a flight crew. Title 14 CFR Section 121.542 restricts communication during critical phases of flight; these phases include all ground operations involving taxi, takeoff, and landing, and all other flight operations conducted below 10,000 feet.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: All the following are methods transport team members can utilize to improve performance under stress, except:
rationale: Seeking the perfect solution is a characteristic often found in novice team members; it is the tendency to seek the perfect solution or diagnosis at the exclusion of other appropriate and safe actions. This tendency may prevent any action from being taken and may allow the emergency to worsen. The other choices are viable ways to improve performance in stressful situations that arise during emergency transport.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What is an Incident Command System (ICS)?
options: program designed to coordinate requests for aircraft and ground responses
management method designed to clarify command relationships and facilitate communication during an emergency
department responsible for planning and coordinating emergency transportation incidents
dedicated facility wherein all tactical operational decisions are made
rationale: The ICS is a management method designed to clarify command relationships at incidents, foster interagency cooperation, and offer maximum flexibility for achieving strategic goals. The functional areas of the ICS include command, operations, planning, logistics, and finance/administration. Field personnel such as transport team members are usually considered part of the operations. The Department of Transportation (DOT) is responsible for planning and coordinating emergency transportation incidents. A communications specialist (CS) is designed to coordinate requests for aircraft and ground responses; it is a title assigned to a person. An Operations Control Center (OCC) is a facility wherein all tactical operational decisions are made.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: While packaging a patient for medical air transport from the scene of crime during which the patient was shot in the head, you discover a handgun. What is the next best step in this scenario?
options: Remove the ammunition from the weapon and ask a crew member to position the weapon in a neutral position for photographing
Take a photo of the weapon in the location where it was found ensuring that reference points can be identified in the photo
Notify law enforcement of the weapon, then pick up the weapon using the grips and move it into a neutral location so the patient can be safely transported
Leave the weapon where it is, notify law enforcement, and then transport the patient around the location of the weapon
rationale: Members of medical air transport crews may at times encounter firearms (or other weapons) at the scene of the transport site. When at all possible, the weapon should be left in place and law enforcement should be notified. The medical air transport crew should always assume firearms are loaded and able to operate correctly, even if the weapon appears to be damaged or is not fully intact. If the weapon needs to be relocated in order to provide medical care to the victim or in order to ensure safe transport of the victim, the weapon should be photographed in place, making sure to include as many of the details of the surrounding environment as possible. Do not attempt to remove ammunition from firearms, but a count of any ammunition visible in the cylinder chamber or the presence and location of any expended ammunition should be made. Only one individual should be responsible for the actual touching of the firearm, and should pick up the weapon using the grips held between the fingers, without concern of obscuring any possible fingerprints.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What are the signs and symptoms of a person in the compensatory phase of hypoxia?
options: Slurred speech, cyanosis, sleepiness, decreased muscle coordination
Increased heart rate, increased respirations, slowed thinking
rationale: Compensatory hypoxia occurs between 10,000 and 15,000 ft altitude.
